title: Dog Asymmetrical Testicles: Causes and When to See a Vet
meta_desc: Noticed one testicle is larger than the other? Learn about the causes of asymmetry in dogs, the urgency level, and how to help your vet.
content_html:
What is Asymmetrical Testicle Size?

Asymmetrical testicle size, or testicular asymmetry, occurs when one of a dog's testicles is visibly larger, firmer, or shaped differently than the other. While slight variations can be normal, a noticeable difference often indicates an underlying medical condition such as inflammation, a cyst, or a growth. It is most commonly seen in intact (un-neutered) male dogs and can affect any breed or age group.

Is it an Emergency?

The urgency level for this condition is Medium. In most cases, asymmetrical testicles are not an immediate life-threatening emergency, but they do require a veterinary examination within 24 to 48 hours. However, if your dog is also experiencing acute pain, lethargy, vomiting, or if the scrotum is extremely red and hot, you should seek veterinary care sooner, as these could be signs of an infection or a painful condition called testicular torsion.

Why a Photo Helps Triage

Capturing a clear photo of the area can be a vital tool for your veterinary team. Because dogs often become tense or tuck their tails during a physical exam, a photo taken in a relaxed environment allows the vet to see the true degree of swelling or displacement. Furthermore, photos provide a visual baseline that helps track if the size is changing rapidly, which is critical information for determining the best course of treatment.

faqs.question: Can testicular asymmetry be a sign of cancer?
faqs.answer: Yes, testicular tumors are a common cause of enlargement in older intact dogs. Most of these tumors are localized and can be cured by neutering the dog.

faqs.question: Is an infection a possible cause?
faqs.answer: Yes, conditions like orchitis (infection of the testicle) or epididymitis (inflammation of the tube behind the testicle) can cause significant swelling and pain.

faqs.question: Will my dog need to be neutered?
faqs.answer: In many cases, neutering is the recommended treatment for testicular asymmetry as it removes the affected tissue and prevents future complications.
